FORM.ACR_UAI_MISSING_ROLE: The identity '{{name}}' does not have the AcrPull role on this Container Registry.
FORM.ACR_NO_UAI: No UAI available in this project.
FORM.ACR_NO_UAI_MSG: No managed identity with the AcrPull role is assigned. Select or create a User Assigned Identity.
FORM.ACR_WHY_UAI: Why use a User Assigned Identity?
FORM.ACR_UAI_CALLOUT_TEXT: With a System-Assigned Identity (SAI), the identity is created at resource deployment time. The AcrPull role assignment then takes a few seconds to several minutes to propagate in Azure. During this window, if the Function App, Web App or Container App attempts to pull the image, Azure returns an authorization error and the resource fails to start. A User Assigned Identity (UAI) is created independently and its AcrPull role can be configured in advance, completely eliminating this propagation delay.
FORM.ACR_UAI_CALLOUT_LINK: Microsoft documentation — Managed Identity best practices

SWITCHER.BOOTSTRAP_INFO: Bootstrap is split: the infra repo owns environments and variable groups; the code repo only validates them and provisions its own application pipelines.
SWITCHER.BOOTSTRAP_APP_INFO: This bootstrap pipeline runs in the code repository. It does NOT create environments or variable groups (those belong to the infra bootstrap). It validates that they exist, then provisions the application pipeline definitions.
SWITCHER.BOOTSTRAP_APP_STEP_1: Commit this YAML in the application repository, then create or update the Azure DevOps bootstrap pipeline from it.
SWITCHER.BOOTSTRAP_APP_STEP_2: Run it only after the infra bootstrap has already created the shared environments and variable groups.
SWITCHER.BOOTSTRAP_APP_STEP_3: Use it to validate those shared resources and provision the application pipeline definitions.
SWITCHER.INFRA_PUSH_DESC: Pushes generated infrastructure artifacts: Bicep, infra pipeline, and infra bootstrap (owner of environments and variable groups).
SWITCHER.CODE_PUSH_DESC: Pushes generated application pipeline artifacts to the code repository. Use the code bootstrap tab below to create or refresh the bootstrap pipeline manually.

BOOTSTRAP.GENERATE_ERROR: Unable to generate the bootstrap. Make sure your project has an Azure DevOps Git configuration.
BOOTSTRAP.GUIDE_TITLE: Full procedure — Azure DevOps first-time setup
BOOTSTRAP.GUIDE_INTRO: Follow these 10 steps in order, once per project. At the end, your CI, PR, Release pipelines and Azure DevOps environments will be ready to use.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_1: Go to the 'Git' tab of this project and make sure an Azure DevOps repository is linked. If not, set it up first (repository URL, default branch).
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_2: Come back to this 'Bootstrap ADO' tab and click the Generate Bootstrap button above. This creates the bootstrap.pipeline.yml file.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_3: Then click 'Push to Git' to send bootstrap.pipeline.yml to the linked Azure DevOps repository. Azure DevOps must see this file in Git before it can create a pipeline from it.
BOOTSTRAP.SECTION_RUN: B — Create and run the pipeline in Azure DevOps
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_4: Open Azure DevOps in your browser. Go to Pipelines > Pipelines, then click New pipeline. Choose Azure Repos Git, select your repository, then choose Existing Azure Pipelines YAML file. Select the branch and the path bootstrap.pipeline.yml, then click Run.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_5: Wait for the run to complete. If the pipeline fails with permission errors on pipelines, Library, or Environments, that is normal the first time — continue to the next section.
BOOTSTRAP.SECTION_PERMISSIONS: C — Grant permissions to the Build Service (if needed)
BOOTSTRAP.PERMISSIONS_HINT: The account to authorize is usually the project-scoped Build Service. Search first for '{Project Name} Build Service ({Organization Name})'; that is the identity to use for the bootstrap permissions, including the Environments hub Creator role required to create environments. For example: 'Infra Flow Sculptor Build Service (floriandrevet0332)'. Only fall back to 'Project Collection Build Service ({Organization Name})' if your project uses collection scope.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_6: Open Pipelines > Pipelines in Azure DevOps. Click the three dots '⋯' at the top-right of the pipelines page, then click Manage security. Search first for '{Project Name} Build Service ({Organization Name})' and add that account. If your project uses collection scope, use 'Project Collection Build Service ({Organization Name})' instead. Grant at least Create build pipeline. If your pipelines are generated in folders (e.g. \Core), make sure the permission applies to the target folder, or set it at the root for all subfolders to inherit.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_7: Open Pipelines > Library in Azure DevOps. Click Security at the top of the page. Add the same Build Service account. If the bootstrap only needs to create new variable groups, the minimum role is Creator. If it also needs to modify existing groups or manage their variables, give it Administrator instead. If some variable groups existed before the bootstrap, make sure it has rights on those too.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_8: Open Pipelines > Environments in Azure DevOps. From the Environments hub security settings, grant the same Build Service account the Environments hub Creator role: this is the permission the bootstrap needs to create Azure DevOps environments. If it does not already inherit that right through Build Administrators or Project Administrators, add it explicitly. Then re-run the bootstrap pipeline. It will create the CI, PR, Release pipelines, the variable groups, and the Azure DevOps environments matching your project environment short names.
BOOTSTRAP.SECTION_FINALIZE: D — Finalize and use the pipelines
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_9: Open Pipelines > Library in Azure DevOps. Open each variable group created by the bootstrap and fill in the secret values manually (passwords, keys, connection strings). These values are empty by default because they must not be stored in the YAML file.
BOOTSTRAP.STEP_10: Open Pipelines > Environments. For each environment marked as requiring approval in InfraFlowSculptor, manually add the intended users or groups in Approvals and checks. The bootstrap now creates the environment automatically, but it still cannot infer approver identities from the app. Once that is done, your pipelines are ready for the normal project workflow.

FIELDS.ACR_NO_UAI_MSG: No managed identity with the AcrPull role is assigned. Select or create a User Assigned Identity.
FIELDS.ACR_WHY_UAI: Why use a User Assigned Identity?
FIELDS.ACR_UAI_CALLOUT_TEXT: With a System-Assigned Identity (SAI), the identity is created at resource deployment time. The AcrPull role assignment then takes a few seconds to several minutes to propagate in Azure. During this window, if the Function App, Web App or Container App attempts to pull the image, Azure returns an authorization error and the resource fails to start. A User Assigned Identity (UAI) is created independently and its AcrPull role can be configured in advance, completely eliminating this propagation delay.
FIELDS.ACR_UAI_CALLOUT_LINK: Microsoft documentation — Managed Identity best practices

ROLE_ASSIGNMENT_IMPACT.TITLE: Impact Analysis
ROLE_ASSIGNMENT_IMPACT.HAS_IMPACT_WARNING: Removing this role assignment may cause the following issues:

IMPACT_TYPE.AcrPullRequired: '{{affectedResource}}' uses container images from '{{targetResource}}'. Removing AcrPull will prevent it from pulling its Docker image.
IMPACT_TYPE.KeyVaultSecretsRequired: '{{affectedResource}}' references {{count}} secret(s) from '{{targetResource}}'. Removing this role will break those secret accesses.

CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_TUTORIAL_TITLE: How to configure your DNS — Step by step guide
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP1_DESC: Open the Azure Portal → navigate to your resource (App Service, Container App, or Function App) → on the Overview page, copy the "Default domain" (e.g., myapp.azurewebsites.net or myapp.happyocean-abc123.westeurope.azurecontainerapps.io).
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP2_TITLE: Get the Custom Domain Verification ID
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP2_DESC: In your resource's left menu, go to "Custom domains" → copy the "Custom Domain Verification ID" shown at the top of the page. This is a unique identifier used to prove ownership of the domain.
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP3_TITLE: Create a CNAME record in your DNS provider
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP3_DESC: In your DNS provider's management console, create a CNAME record: Host = your subdomain (e.g., "api" for api.example.com) → Value = the default hostname copied in step 1. This redirects traffic to your Azure resource.
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP4_DESC: In your DNS provider, create a TXT record: Host = "asuid." followed by your subdomain (e.g., "asuid.api" for api.example.com) → Value = the Custom Domain Verification ID copied in step 2. This proves you own the domain.
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP5_TITLE: Add your domain here and wait for validation
CUSTOM_DOMAINS.DNS_STEP5_DESC: Once the DNS records have propagated (this can take a few minutes to 48 hours), come back here and add your domain using the button above. Azure will automatically verify the DNS records and provision a managed TLS certificate.

BLOB_CORS.TITLE: Blob service CORS
BLOB_CORS.DESCRIPTION: Define origins, methods, headers, and cache duration applied to the blob service.

TABLE_CORS.TITLE: Table service CORS
TABLE_CORS.DESCRIPTION: Define origins, methods, headers, and cache duration applied to the table service.
TABLE_CORS.EMPTY: No CORS rule configured for the table service.

CORS_COMMON.ORIGINS_HINT: Use * or an exact http/https origin. Wildcard subdomains such as https://*.contoso.com are supported.
CORS_COMMON.METHODS_HINT: Select only the HTTP verbs your client really needs. The most restrictive rule set is the safest.
CORS_COMMON.ALLOWED_HEADERS_HINT: Add only the request headers your client actually sends. Prefixes ending with * help reduce deployment errors.
CORS_COMMON.EXPOSED_HEADERS_HINT: Expose only the response headers the browser must read. Prefer focused values or an x-ms-* prefix.
CORS_COMMON.MAX_AGE_HINT: Browser preflight cache duration. 3600 seconds is a practical starting point.
CORS_COMMON.FIX_VALIDATION_ERRORS: Fix the CORS errors before saving.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_INVALID_ORIGIN: Enter * or a valid http/https origin without path or query string.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_ORIGIN_REQUIRED: Add at least one allowed origin.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_METHOD_REQUIRED: Select at least one allowed method.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_INVALID_HEADER: Enter a valid HTTP header name. The * wildcard is only allowed as a suffix.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_MAX_AGE: Max age must be a whole number greater than or equal to 0.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_DUPLICATE_VALUE: This value is already present in the rule.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_TOO_MANY_PREFIX_HEADERS: You cannot define more than 2 prefixed headers ending with *.
CORS_COMMON.ERROR_TOO_MANY_LITERAL_HEADERS: You cannot define more than 64 literal headers per list.
